title: Reading and Use of English - Part 2
description: For questions 9 - 16, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only one word in each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).

content.text: Citizen science is the practice of engaging members of the public in the collection and analysis of scientific data, (0) which has expanded significantly with the growth of digital platforms and mobile technology. It offers a model for scaling research beyond the resources of professional institutions. (9) Although the involvement of amateur observers in scientific data collection is not entirely new, having roots in the naturalist traditions of the eighteenth century, the scope and ambition of contemporary citizen science projects are genuinely unprecedented. (10) Such is the volume of data that can be gathered through coordinated public participation that some projects have achieved in weeks what would have taken professional researchers years to accomplish alone. These projects span a wide range of disciplines, and (11) Provided that participants receive adequate training and clear protocols, the data they produce can meet rigorous quality standards. Not (12) only does citizen science extend the geographic and temporal range of scientific observation, but it also creates direct engagement between the public and research processes. (13) Regardless of the discipline, successful citizen science projects share a commitment to transparency about how data will be used and how contributors will be recognised. Not (14) until digital platforms made large-scale coordination straightforward did citizen science move from a marginal curiosity to a mainstream research methodology. (15) Now that reliable mobile applications can guide non-specialists through data collection, the barriers to meaningful public participation in research have fallen dramatically. Projects in fields from bird monitoring to galaxy classification continue to demonstrate the model's potential. (16) Whether citizen science is approached primarily as a means of generating data or as a way of building public understanding of the scientific process, its value is increasingly recognised by funding bodies and research institutions.

example.text: Citizen science is the practice of engaging members of the public in the collection and analysis of scientific data, (0) which has expanded significantly with the growth of digital platforms and mobile technology.

questions.explanation: Concessive conjunction used to introduce a contrast or concession between two clauses.
Emphatic determiner in the inverted structure 'Such was/is the [noun] that...' to express a high degree.
Conditional conjunction meaning 'on the condition that', used to introduce a condition and its result.
Part of the correlative conjunction 'Not only... but also', which requires subject-auxiliary inversion after 'Not only'.
Part of the fixed prepositional phrase 'Regardless of', meaning 'without being affected by'.
Part of the 'Not until' negative fronting structure, which requires subject-auxiliary inversion.
Temporal/causal conjunction in the structure 'Now that', meaning 'since a new situation has been reached', introducing a causal or temporal clause.
Conjunction introducing two alternatives or possibilities in a summarising conclusion.
